description: Scoring rubric for emails and LinkedIn messages generated by the NEA Outreach Agent.
quality_bar: Would the investor send this message unedited?
composite: weighted_average of dimension scores, rounded to 1 decimal place

context_tags.note: Sparse-data companies hit cold_with_firm_context fallback; penalizing weak personalization is unfair when data is unavailable.
Day-0 outputs should be scored more leniently than Day-60 ones.
Investor-provided outreach_goal, event details, or prior relationship context improve output relevance.
Voice fidelity is investor-specific. A correct output for Madison is wrong for Danielle.
LinkedIn has a ~300-character practical limit that is not system-enforced.

global_scale.label: Excellent
global_scale.definition: Investor would send this message unedited. No detectable AI tells, strong personalization, perfect voice match.

global_scale.label: Good
global_scale.definition: Minor adjustments needed (e.g., slightly wrong length, one weak signal choice). Investor would edit 1-2 sentences before sending.

global_scale.label: Acceptable
global_scale.definition: Structurally correct and on-voice, but personalization is shallow or partially generic. Investor would need to rewrite a paragraph before sending.

global_scale.label: Below Standard
global_scale.definition: Noticeable problems: wrong voice register, AI tells present, weak structure. Investor would not send this without major edits.

global_scale.label: Poor
global_scale.definition: Fundamental failures: wrong investor voice, fabricated claims, wrong contact, or structurally incoherent. Requires complete rewrite.

dimensions.name: Voice Fidelity
dimensions.description: Does the message sound like it was written by this specific investor, not a generic VC or a different investor on the team?

criteria.name: Greeting style matches investor profile

anchors.5: Madison uses em-dash greeting ('James—'); Danielle uses warm 'Hey [Name],'; Tiffany/Ashley use 'Hi [Name],'
anchors.3: Correct formality level but not the investor's specific pattern.
anchors.1: Wrong style entirely (e.g., 'Hi James,' from Madison, or em-dash from Danielle).

criteria.name: Message length is appropriate for investor + context type

anchors.5: Madison 4-6 sentences; Ashley length proportional to data richness; Tiffany medium; Danielle medium-long with firm block.
anchors.3: Within ±30% of expected length.
anchors.1: Gross mismatch (Madison at 300+ words; Ashley at 3 sentences on a thesis topic).

criteria.name: Formality register matches investor voice

anchors.5: Danielle casual/warm; Tiffany professional/efficient; Ashley intellectually warm; Madison concise/credential-forward.
anchors.3: Register is close but includes 1-2 off-register phrases.
anchors.1: Register is wrong (e.g., corporate tone for Danielle, casual for Tiffany).

criteria.name: Ask style matches investor pattern

anchors.5: Ashley = soft collaborative; Tiffany = concrete + NYC option; Danielle = inclusive team; Madison = one direct sentence.
anchors.3: Ask is present and reasonable but uses generic phrasing rather than the investor's pattern.
anchors.1: Wrong ask style entirely or missing ask.

criteria.name: Sign-off matches investor conventions

anchors.5: Correct sign-off name and formality (e.g., 'Best, Ashley' or just 'Ashley').
anchors.3: Name is correct but formality slightly off.
anchors.1: Wrong name, missing sign-off, or grossly wrong formality.

criteria.name: Firm context block usage is correct

anchors.5: Danielle appends full firm context block; Madison includes when data sparse; Ashley/Tiffany generally omit.
anchors.3: Block present/absent with minor deviation from expected pattern.
anchors.1: Block included when it shouldn't be, or missing when required (Danielle).

dimensions.name: Personalization Depth
dimensions.description: How specific is the personalization to this founder and company? Scored against the system's 7-tier priority hierarchy.

personalization_hierarchy.example: Architecture choice, named feature, design decision
Shows homework beyond the company page
Same employer, university, research area (must be verifiable)
Funding, launch, hire, partnership (timely and concrete)
Investor has active thesis that fits; connection explained
Complementary portfolio company with meaningful connection

criteria.name: Highest-tier signal is used from available data

anchors.5: Uses highest available tier (e.g., P1 technical detail when data supports it).
anchors.3: Uses a mid-tier signal when a higher-tier was available in the data.
anchors.1: Uses only P6-P7 signals when P1-P3 data was available, or no personalization at all.

criteria.name: Signal is specific to THIS founder/company

anchors.5: Reference would only make sense for this specific company (e.g., a named product feature).
anchors.3: Reference is sector-appropriate but could apply to 5+ companies.
anchors.1: Reference is fully generic ('impressive product', 'love what you're building').

criteria.name: Every personalization claim is traceable to provided data

anchors.5: Each signal can be mapped to a specific field in the CompanyBundle.
anchors.3: Most signals are traceable; one is a reasonable inference.
anchors.1: One or more signals appear fabricated or unsupported by data.

criteria.name: Portfolio mentions include a meaningful connection

anchors.5: Portfolio company is named with a clear, specific reason it's relevant.
anchors.3: Portfolio company named with a vague connection ('we've invested in this space').
anchors.1: Portfolio dropped without any connection to the founder's company (explicit anti-pattern).

dimensions.name: Context Type Appropriateness
dimensions.description: Was the correct context type selected for the available data and investor intent? Evaluates an upstream pipeline decision, not the LLM's writing quality.

criteria.name: Context type matches the strongest available signal

anchors.5: Best-fit type selected (e.g., cold_funding_congrats fires when funding_announcement is the top signal).
anchors.3: Reasonable type but not optimal (e.g., cold_technical_interest when thesis_driven_deep_dive was better).
anchors.1: Clearly wrong type (e.g., cold_personal_affinity for a deep-tech infrastructure company).

criteria.name: Warm/event types fire when investor context is provided

anchors.5: has_prior_relationship=True correctly triggers warm type; has_event_context=True correctly triggers event type.
anchors.3: N/A (binary: either the flag was set and it fired, or it wasn't).
anchors.1: Flag was set but warm/event type did not fire (pipeline bug).

criteria.name: Fallback (cold_with_firm_context) did not fire when richer signals were available

anchors.5: Non-fallback type selected with clear justification from signals.
anchors.3: Fallback fired but signals were only marginally above threshold.
anchors.1: Fallback fired despite multiple strong signals being present.

criteria.name: Opening move matches context type

anchors.5: Opens correctly (e.g., hook for thesis_driven_deep_dive, congrats for cold_funding_congrats, credential for cold_market_gap).
anchors.3: Opens with a relevant but non-canonical move.
anchors.1: Opens with the wrong type entirely (credential when it should be a hook, or generic when it should be congrats).

criteria.name: Body sequencing follows the email pattern

anchors.5: All body elements appear in the correct order per the context type's email_pattern.
anchors.3: Elements present but in slightly wrong order.
anchors.1: Key structural elements missing or misordered.

criteria.name: Self-introduction is correctly positioned

anchors.5: Self-intro comes second (not first) for Ashley; comes first for Tiffany; credential-forward for Madison.
anchors.3: Self-intro present but positioned one step off.
anchors.1: Self-intro missing or in a position that breaks the email's logic.

criteria.name: Subject line present and appropriate (email) / LinkedIn length respected (LinkedIn)

email.5: Subject line present, specific, and non-generic.
email.3: Subject line present but somewhat generic.
email.1: Subject line missing (parsing failure) or misleading.

linkedin.5: LinkedIn message is concise, under ~300 characters for preview readability.
linkedin.3: Slightly over preview limit but still readable.
linkedin.1: LinkedIn message is email-length with no format adaptation.
linkedin.na: Score N/A if format is email.

dimensions.name: AI-Tell Absence
dimensions.description: Each detected AI tell deducts from a starting score of 5. Detectable AI patterns destroy credibility with sophisticated founders.
dimensions.scoring_method: Start at 5. Deduct 1 point for each major violation and 0.5 for each minor violation. Floor at 1.

violations.tell: Em-dash used mid-sentence (not in Madison greeting)
violations.example: 'The architecture — combining retrieval with traversal — is compelling'
'I came across your company and was impressed'
'As a former Meta researcher, you understand...'
Technical claim not in the CompanyBundle data

violations.tell: Exclamation inflation (2+ more than investor norm)
violations.example: 5 exclamation marks when investor style uses 0-1
'I hope this message finds you well' from Danielle

dimensions.name: Factual Accuracy
dimensions.description: Are all claims in the email traceable to the actual data provided in the CompanyBundle?
dimensions.scoring_method: Start at 5. Deduct 2 points for each major factual error and 1 point for each minor error. Floor at 1. A single factual error triggers an automatic flag.

criteria.name: No fabricated technical details or product claims

anchors.major: Technical architecture, feature, or capability mentioned that is not in the data. Deduct 2.
anchors.minor: Slightly embellished description that is a reasonable inference but not directly stated. Deduct 1.

criteria.name: No incorrect portfolio claims

anchors.major: Portfolio company attributed to NEA that is not in the investor's portfolio list. Deduct 2.
anchors.minor: Correct portfolio company but wrong context (e.g., wrong round or wrong relationship). Deduct 1.

criteria.name: No wrong funding round details

anchors.major: Wrong round amount, date, or investor attribution. Deduct 2.
anchors.minor: Ambiguous phrasing that could be misread (e.g., 'recent' for a 6-month-old round). Deduct 1.

criteria.name: Contact name and title are correct

anchors.major: Wrong person addressed, or wrong title used. Deduct 2.
anchors.minor: Title slightly off (e.g., 'Co-Founder' vs. 'Co-Founder & CEO'). Deduct 1.

criteria.name: News references are real and correctly characterized

anchors.major: Reference to a news event that does not exist in the provided data. Deduct 2.
anchors.minor: Real event but slightly mischaracterized. Deduct 1.

dimensions.name: Technical Quality
dimensions.description: Applies primarily to Ashley and Madison. For Danielle, score N/A and redistribute weight. For Tiffany, score only if the context type is technical (thesis_driven_deep_dive, cold_technical_interest).

conditional.Tiffany: applies only for thesis_driven_deep_dive or cold_technical_interest context types

criteria.name: Technical terms are used correctly and specifically

anchors.5: Terms are precise and demonstrate genuine understanding (e.g., 'retrieval-augmented generation' not just 'AI').
anchors.3: Terms are correct but generic (e.g., 'machine learning' when a more specific term applies).
anchors.1: Terms are misused, conflated, or buzzword-stuffed.

criteria.name: Thesis connection is coherent

anchors.5: Clear, specific link between the product's architecture and the investor's focus area.
anchors.3: Thesis connection is present but vague.
anchors.1: No thesis connection, or the connection is logically incoherent.

criteria.name: Technical depth matches available data

anchors.5: Deep-dive when data is rich; appropriately surface-level when data is sparse.
anchors.3: Slightly too deep (overreaching from limited data) or too shallow (ignoring available detail).
anchors.1: Grossly mismatched depth (hallucinated architecture when data is sparse, or generic when data is rich).

dimensions.name: Actionability
dimensions.description: Would a founder know exactly what to do after reading this email? The email's purpose is to get a reply.

criteria.name: A clear, single ask is present

anchors.5: One unambiguous ask (meeting, call, intro).
anchors.3: Ask is present but buried or ambiguous.
anchors.1: No ask, multiple competing asks, or ask is inappropriate (e.g., 'are you fundraising?' on cold intro).

criteria.name: Ask is correctly calibrated for context

anchors.5: Cold = exploratory meeting; warm = catch-up; event = in-person meetup.
anchors.3: Ask is reasonable but slightly off-caliber.
anchors.1: Ask is wildly miscalibrated (e.g., requesting term sheet on cold outreach).

criteria.name: Ask follows demonstrated understanding

anchors.5: Ask comes after the personalization and homework section, not before.
anchors.3: Ask is positioned acceptably but feels premature.
anchors.1: Ask leads the email with no prior demonstration of research.

automatic_flags.condition: 2 or more AI-tell violations detected in a single output

llm_judge_instructions.scoring_style: smooth (1-5 range per dimension, no forced pass/fail on individual criteria)

violations_detected: list of factual error IDs with major/minor classification
